article.title: Characterization of novel regulators for heat stress tolerance in tomato from Indian sub‐continent

events.event_label: HS up‑regulates HSFA6a and down‑regulates HSFA4b
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s006: The Arabidopsis hsfb1‐hsfb2b double knockout mutant demonstrates higher acquired thermotolerance (Ikeda et al.,; Kumar et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s007: HSFA6a was up‐regulated, and HSFA4b was down‐regulated under HS only in CLN but not in CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s008: AtHSFA6a is induced in response to ABA, salt and drought and is responsible for the transcriptional activation of stress‐responsive gene DREB2a via ABA‐dependent signalling pathway (Hwang et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s009: Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s010: Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ).

events.event_label: ERFs act as positive regulators of HS response and improve thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s018: Our data on TF regulation dynamics highlight role of TF families other than the undisputed relevance of HSFs in HS response. PMC7540533.p022.s019: These TFs could be promising candidates for enhancing tomato thermotolerance as we demonstrated for the three ERFs that function as positive regulators of HS response.

events.event_label: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 are expressed in tomato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s005: While Solyc09g014280 is induced upon potato cyst nematode infection in tomato roots (Swiecicka et al.,), Solyc05g052670 is up‐regulated in tomato in response to tomato yellow leaf curl virus infection (Chen et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s006: Here, we report yet unexplored function of an ASAT member in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p026.s007: Plants exhibiting successful knock‐down of Solyc09g014280 transcripts (Figure S11) showed severe drooping of leaves upon recovery after being exposed to HS in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4a).

events.event_label: DAB and Trypan blue staining detect leaves under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p027.s006: Survival was gauged 6 days postrecovery. PMC7540533.p027.s007: (b) DAB and Trypan blue staining of leaves of HS‐treated empty vector (TRV), TRV‐ASAT and TRV‐Notabilis‐silenced plants. PMC7540533.p027.s008: (c–f) Estimation of net photosynthesis rate (μmol/m2/s), water‐use efficiency (mmol/mol), transpiration rate (mmol/m2/s) and stomatal conductance (mol/m2/s) in empty vector (TRV), TRV‐ASAT and TRV‐Notabilis‐silenced plants following heat stress. PMC7540533.p027.s009: (g,h) Estimation of survival (percentage) and percentage reduction in hypocotyl length of seedlings under control conditions and after 5 days of heat stress in plants with overexpression of empty vector, ASAT and Notabilis seedlings. PMC7540533.p027.s010: Data are means of four biological sets of 70 seedlings each. (i) Expression profiles of HSR genes in heat, ABA and VIGS silenced ASAT and Notabilis plants by qRT‐PCR.

events.event_label: Notabilis regulates ABA biosynthesis pathway and correlates positively with water stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s001: We then functionally validated the other tolerant‐up gene, Solyc07g056570 or Notabilis (9‐cis‐epoxycarotenoid dioxygenase 1). PMC7540533.p029.s002: Notabilis regulates the rate‐limiting step in the ABA biosynthesis pathway and has important role in water stress tolerance as evidenced by several reports (Burbidge et al.,; He et al.,; Luchi et al.,; Qin and Zeevaart,; Wan and Li,). PMC7540533.p029.s003: In Arabidopsis, ABA‐deficient and signalling mutants are involved in acquired thermotolerance acquisition (Larkindale and Huang,). PMC7540533.p029.s004: Arabidopsis thaliana NGATHA1 induces ABA biosynthesis by activating Notabilis homolog NCED3 gene during dehydration stress (Sato et al.,).

events.event_label: NGATHA1 activates ABA biosynthesis by inducing NCED3
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s003: In Arabidopsis, ABA‐deficient and signalling mutants are involved in acquired thermotolerance acquisition (Larkindale and Huang,). PMC7540533.p029.s004: Arabidopsis thaliana NGATHA1 induces ABA biosynthesis by activating Notabilis homolog NCED3 gene during dehydration stress (Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s005: In lettuce, its homolog (NCED4) is the causal gene in the Htg6.1 QTL, associated with thermo‐inhibition of seed germination (Huo et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a).

events.event_label: NCED6 and NCED9 enhance thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p029.s008: This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst.

events.event_label: PI‑II inhibits trypsin and chymotrypsin
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s001: Furthermore, another gene but a ‘sensitive‐up gene’ which is Solyc03g020030 was functionally characterized. PMC7540533.p031.s002: This belongs to the proteinase inhibitor‐II (PI‐II) serine‐PI family having dimeric domains in the protein, inhibiting trypsin and chymotrypsin (Tamhane et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s003: The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s004: Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,).

events.event_label: HS alters expression of Solyc03g020030
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s003: The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s004: Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s005: Only a Kunitz PI (another class of serine‐PI family) has been shown to be induced in response to heat stress (Annamalai and Yanagihara,), however, information regarding PI‐II‐class role in HS is completely lacking. PMC7540533.p031.s006: Solyc03g020030 was expressed at high levels in CLN under control condition but decreased up to threefold under HS, while significant increase was noticed in CA4 under HS. PMC7540533.p031.s007: We find there is enrichment of GO term of ‘serine‐type endopeptidase inhibitor activity’ in CA4‐specific up‐regulation (Figure 2c).

events.event_label: PI‑II knock‑down enhances WUEi and net photosynthetic rates
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). PMC7540533.p031.s010: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c). PMC7540533.p031.s011: In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d). PMC7540533.p031.s012: The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings. PMC7540533.p031.s013: In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g).

events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). PMC7540533.p031.s011: In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d). PMC7540533.p031.s012: The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings. PMC7540533.p031.s013: In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g).

events.event_label: PI‑II overexpression reduces hypocotyl length and survival rate
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). PMC7540533.p031.s010: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c). PMC7540533.p031.s011: In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d). PMC7540533.p031.s012: The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings. PMC7540533.p031.s013: In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g).

events.event_label: HS regulates expression of ERF.F4, ERF.F5 and ERF.C1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s012: Of these, 12 ERFs were similarly regulated (9 up‐regulated and 3‐regulated down) in both cultivars, while others were regulated in cultivar‐biased manner. PMC7540533.p022.s013: We selected three previously uncharacterized ERFs (ERF.F4, ERF.F5, and ERF.C1) in HS response based on the observation that they all maintained high levels in CLN and low levels in CA4 under HS. PMC7540533.p022.s014: This was validated by qRT‐PCR also (Figure 3e). PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5.

events.event_label: TaNCED overexpression enhances drought tolerance and reduces stomatal conductance and transpiration
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s012: This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS. PMC7540533.p029.s013: The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s014: There was a significant reduction in the photosynthetic rate and water‐use efficiency along with a significant rise in stomatal conductance and enhanced transpiration rate in silenced plants in contrast to vector control plants (Figure 4c–f). PMC7540533.p029.s015: Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s016: Further proof for Notabilis as a gene imparting thermotolerance was provided from tomato plants overexpressing Notabilis as judged by better seedling survival percentage and hypocotyl length in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g,h).

events.event_label: HSFA2a and HSFA7a participate in Notabilis‑ABA pathway and contribute to thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s018: Moreover, when we checked the expression of these HSR genes in response to ABA, they were all up‐regulated, suggesting Notabilis as an upstream regulator of these HS marker genes (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s019: Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s020: Furthermore, we asked whether the opposite expression of Notabilis in CLN‐CA4 cultivars is because of variation in promoter sequences and/or an inherent difference in TF pool of both the cultivars. PMC7540533.p029.s021: Sequence information of promoters for both the varieties highlighted no SNPs; thus, a regulatory influence of the cis‐sites in the contrasting expression was ruled out. PMC7540533.p029.s022: We then checked the expression of GUS gene hooked to Notabilis promoter in CLN and CA4 background.

events.event_label: ASAT enzymes catalyze synthesis of Acylsugars from sucrose and acyl‑CoA donors in tip cells
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

events.event_label: Overexpression of ERF.F4 and ERF.F5 enhances hypocotyl length, shoot, root and total seedling weight
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5. PMC7540533.p022.s017: Seedlings overexpressing ERF.F4 and F5 exhibited higher hypocotyl length as well as shoot, root and total seedling weight as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3g,h). PMC7540533.p022.s018: Our data on TF regulation dynamics highlight role of TF families other than the undisputed relevance of HSFs in HS response. PMC7540533.p022.s019: These TFs could be promising candidates for enhancing tomato thermotolerance as we demonstrated for the three ERFs that function as positive regulators of HS response.

events.event_label: HSFA1–HSFBs negative feedback loop
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s011: Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

events.event_label: Proteinase inhibitors and HXXXD‑type acyltransferases participate in plant defence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p014.s009: Solyc01g065530 (protein COBRA), Solyc01g081250 (glutathione s‐transferase), Solyc01g087020 (transmembrane protein), Solyc01g095140 (late embryogenesis abundant protein), Solyc01g107780 (glycosyltransferase), Solyc03g116890 (WRKY transcription factor 39), Soly05g052670, Solyc05g052680 and Solyc09g014280 (all three HXXXD‐type acyltransferase family proteins), Solyc06g049070 (nucleotide/sugar transporter family protein), Solyc07g008103 (blue copper protein), Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis), Solyc07g064410 (Kua‐ubiquitin conjugating enzyme hybrid), Solyc09g075920 (serine/threonine‐protein kinase), Solyc09g092500 (glycosyltransferase) and Solyc10g081570 (Marmande), were up‐regulated in CLN but down‐regulated in CA4 upon HS. PMC7540533.p014.s010: Gene ontology (GO) enrichment of these antagonistic genes highlighted the significant enrichment of negative regulation of endopeptidase, peptidase and proteolysis, response to water, pollination and transferase activity (Figure 2c). PMC7540533.p014.s011: Among these, five (the two proteinase inhibitors and the three HXXXD‐type acyltransferases) have roles in plant defence. PMC7540533.p014.s012: The qRT‐PCR validation of 10 genes selected from the above clusters confirmed the opposite HS regulation in CA4 and CLN (Figure 2d).

events.event_label: Heat stress up‑regulates HSFA2 and other HSFs in tomato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated.

events.event_label: bZIP28, HSFA2 and ROS participate in heat response pathways
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s014: Apart from these canonical HSR circuits, PIF4 regulatory and associated signalling networks control plant thermo‐morphogenesis (Koini et al.,; Oh et al.,; Proveniers and Van Zanten,; Stavang et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s015: The significance of post‐translational regulation in HS response is also well documented in plant HSR (Agarwal et al.,; Hahn et al.,; Miller et al.,; Mizoi et al.,; Sato et al.,; Vainonen et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s016: Evidences also suggest orchestration of multi‐layered regulatory systems comprising not only TFs but also epigenetic regulators and small RNAs as important HSR players.

events.event_label: HSPs, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation contribute to heat stress response in tomato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s001: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) is the second most cultivated vegetable in the world. PMC7540533.p006.s002: HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,).

events.event_label: ZAT12, NAC019, bZIP28 and PIF4 contribute to thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation. PMC7540533.p020.s017: Using these criteria, members belonging to TF families B3, C3H, HSF, NAC and Trihelix were majorly induced in response to HS, while ARF, Dof, MADS, TCP and YABBY were primarily repressed in response to HS (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s018: Thermotolerance in transgenic systems has been achieved by modulating the levels of several TFs like ZAT12 (Shah et al.,), NAC019 (Guan et al.,), bZIP28 (Srivastava et al.,) and PIF4 (a bHLH transcription factor) in Arabidopsis (Gangappa et al.,; Hwang et al.,).

events.event_label: Acylsugars act against whiteflies and spider mites and are restricted to Solanaceae
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

events.event_label: heat stress reduces hypocotyl length and induces leaf staining
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p027.s006: Survival was gauged 6 days postrecovery. PMC7540533.p027.s007: (b) DAB and Trypan blue staining of leaves of HS‐treated empty vector (TRV), TRV‐ASAT and TRV‐Notabilis‐silenced plants. PMC7540533.p027.s008: (c–f) Estimation of net photosynthesis rate (μmol/m2/s), water‐use efficiency (mmol/mol), transpiration rate (mmol/m2/s) and stomatal conductance (mol/m2/s) in empty vector (TRV), TRV‐ASAT and TRV‐Notabilis‐silenced plants following heat stress. PMC7540533.p027.s009: (g,h) Estimation of survival (percentage) and percentage reduction in hypocotyl length of seedlings under control conditions and after 5 days of heat stress in plants with overexpression of empty vector, ASAT and Notabilis seedlings. PMC7540533.p027.s010: Data are means of four biological sets of 70 seedlings each. (i) Expression profiles of HSR genes in heat, ABA and VIGS silenced ASAT and Notabilis plants by qRT‐PCR.

events.event_label: heat stress alters expression of Kunitz PI and Solyc03g020030
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s003: The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s004: Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s005: Only a Kunitz PI (another class of serine‐PI family) has been shown to be induced in response to heat stress (Annamalai and Yanagihara,), however, information regarding PI‐II‐class role in HS is completely lacking. PMC7540533.p031.s006: Solyc03g020030 was expressed at high levels in CLN under control condition but decreased up to threefold under HS, while significant increase was noticed in CA4 under HS. PMC7540533.p031.s007: We find there is enrichment of GO term of ‘serine‐type endopeptidase inhibitor activity’ in CA4‐specific up‐regulation (Figure 2c).

events.event_label: Heat stress up‑regulates HSFA2, DREB and their corresponding genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s009: Of these, 3 TFs, viz. PMC7540533.p020.s010: HSFA2 (Solyc08g062960), and dehydration‐responsive element‐binding (DREB) TF (Solyc05g052410) were more than 100‐fold up‐regulated in both cultivars. PMC7540533.p020.s011: The plot of above 30 TFs clearly revealed that the heat‐tolerant CLN showed higher magnitude of HS‐mediated induction in majority of the TFs and common key TF pool was activated upon HS in both cultivars (Figure 3b). PMC7540533.p020.s012: Moreover, 26 TFs showed HS‐mediated strong repression (FC ≤ 5) in both cultivars (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s013: While Solyc08g008280 (WRKY‐53) was the top repressed candidate in CLN, Solyc10g005080 (late elongated hypocotyl) was highly down‐regulated TF in CA4 under HS (Figure 3c).

events.event_label: Heat stress induces expression of B3, C3H, HSF, NAC and Trihelix families
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation. PMC7540533.p020.s017: Using these criteria, members belonging to TF families B3, C3H, HSF, NAC and Trihelix were majorly induced in response to HS, while ARF, Dof, MADS, TCP and YABBY were primarily repressed in response to HS (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s018: Thermotolerance in transgenic systems has been achieved by modulating the levels of several TFs like ZAT12 (Shah et al.,), NAC019 (Guan et al.,), bZIP28 (Srivastava et al.,) and PIF4 (a bHLH transcription factor) in Arabidopsis (Gangappa et al.,; Hwang et al.,).

events.event_label: HS alters ERF expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s009: Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s010: Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ). PMC7540533.p022.s011: Out of 147 ERFs reported in tomato, 30 members followed differential HS response in CLN and CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s012: Of these, 12 ERFs were similarly regulated (9 up‐regulated and 3‐regulated down) in both cultivars, while others were regulated in cultivar‐biased manner. PMC7540533.p022.s013: We selected three previously uncharacterized ERFs (ERF.F4, ERF.F5, and ERF.C1) in HS response based on the observation that they all maintained high levels in CLN and low levels in CA4 under HS.

events.event_label: HS induces HSF family expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated.

events.event_label: Solyc09g014280 expressed in roots associates with potato cyst nematode; Solyc05g052670 expressed in tomato associates with tomato yellow leaf curl virus
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s005: While Solyc09g014280 is induced upon potato cyst nematode infection in tomato roots (Swiecicka et al.,), Solyc05g052670 is up‐regulated in tomato in response to tomato yellow leaf curl virus infection (Chen et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s006: Here, we report yet unexplored function of an ASAT member in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p026.s007: Plants exhibiting successful knock‐down of Solyc09g014280 transcripts (Figure S11) showed severe drooping of leaves upon recovery after being exposed to HS in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4a).

events.event_label: NAC019 regulates multiple HSFA genes and contributes to thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s011: Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

events.event_label: HS and abiotic signals regulate AtHSFA6a which activates DREB2a
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s006: The Arabidopsis hsfb1‐hsfb2b double knockout mutant demonstrates higher acquired thermotolerance (Ikeda et al.,; Kumar et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s007: HSFA6a was up‐regulated, and HSFA4b was down‐regulated under HS only in CLN but not in CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s008: AtHSFA6a is induced in response to ABA, salt and drought and is responsible for the transcriptional activation of stress‐responsive gene DREB2a via ABA‐dependent signalling pathway (Hwang et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s009: Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s010: Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ).

events.event_label: HSFA2a and HSFA7a participate in Notabilis-ABA pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s018: Moreover, when we checked the expression of these HSR genes in response to ABA, they were all up‐regulated, suggesting Notabilis as an upstream regulator of these HS marker genes (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s019: Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s020: Furthermore, we asked whether the opposite expression of Notabilis in CLN‐CA4 cultivars is because of variation in promoter sequences and/or an inherent difference in TF pool of both the cultivars. PMC7540533.p029.s021: Sequence information of promoters for both the varieties highlighted no SNPs; thus, a regulatory influence of the cis‐sites in the contrasting expression was ruled out. PMC7540533.p029.s022: We then checked the expression of GUS gene hooked to Notabilis promoter in CLN and CA4 background.

events.event_label: ERF.F4 and ERF.F5 increase root growth
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5. PMC7540533.p022.s017: Seedlings overexpressing ERF.F4 and F5 exhibited higher hypocotyl length as well as shoot, root and total seedling weight as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3g,h). PMC7540533.p022.s018: Our data on TF regulation dynamics highlight role of TF families other than the undisputed relevance of HSFs in HS response. PMC7540533.p022.s019: These TFs could be promising candidates for enhancing tomato thermotolerance as we demonstrated for the three ERFs that function as positive regulators of HS response.

PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,).

events.event_label: HSP21 affects PSII

PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s005: Tomato HSFA2 is highly up‐regulated during HS that in turn forms a hetero‐oligomeric transcriptional complex with HSFA1. PMC7540533.p006.s006: In addition, HSP17.4‐CII acts as a co‐repressor of HSFA2 (Port et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s007: Overexpression of Arabidopsis receptor‐like kinase ERECTA (ER) leads to enhanced HS tolerance in Arabidopsis, rice and tomato with increased biomass (Shen et al.,).

events.event_label: HS occurs_in Tomato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s009: Till now, the knowledge about the effect of HS on tomato leaf by exploiting the comparative transcriptomics in contrasting cultivars is not well understood. PMC7540533.p006.s010: Tomato is increasingly becoming a cash crop in regions of higher temperatures (the tropics and sub‐tropics) than the optimum 26/20 °C during the photoperiod/dark period (Srivastava et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s011: One way for developing HS‐resilient crops is to exploit the naturally evolved mechanistic signalling frameworks in cultivars exhibiting contrasting response to HS (Challinor et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s012: To uncover the HS‐responsive molecular mechanisms in tomato using naturally occurring cultivars growing in the sub‐tropical climatic conditions of the Indian sub‐continent, we first evaluated nine tolerant/sensitive tomato cultivars under HS to identify the best contrasting pair, viz. PMC7540533.p006.s013: CLN1621L (henceforth referred to as CLN, tolerant) and CA4 (sensitive).

events.event_label: heat‐responsive is_detected_in leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s012: To uncover the HS‐responsive molecular mechanisms in tomato using naturally occurring cultivars growing in the sub‐tropical climatic conditions of the Indian sub‐continent, we first evaluated nine tolerant/sensitive tomato cultivars under HS to identify the best contrasting pair, viz. PMC7540533.p006.s013: CLN1621L (henceforth referred to as CLN, tolerant) and CA4 (sensitive). PMC7540533.p006.s014: Comparative analysis of the heat‐responsive transcriptomic signatures of leaves highlighted conserved to genotype‐specific reprogramming at transcriptional levels. PMC7540533.p006.s015: Functional assessment by silencing (virus‐induced gene silencing, VIGS) and transient overexpression of three previously uncharacterized genes, that exhibit antagonistic expression in the contrasting cultivars under HS, established their roles as positive/negative regulators of HS tolerance. PMC7540533.p006.s016: Promoter:reporter expression analysis highlights that cultivar‐dependent transcription factors govern the opposite expression of these genes.

events.event_label: Fruit set positively_correlates_with yield
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p009.s009: CLN displayed highest ranking among the cultivars for all the above parameters except EL (Figure 1a and Figure S1e). PMC7540533.p009.s010: CA4 on the other hand had highest EL and obtained lowest rank for all other parameters except proline content where Pusa Ruby had least value (Figure 1a; Figure S1c). PMC7540533.p009.s011: Of note, percentage survival of 1‐month‐old CLN plants was more than 80%, whereas it was mere 11% in CA4. PMC7540533.p009.s012: Fruit set was also higher in CLN (81%) as compared to CA4 (27%), as reflected by nearly 50% yield being maintained in CLN but only 6% in CA4 (Figure S1f,g). PMC7540533.p009.s013: Hierarchical clustering clearly divided the cultivars into four clades based on their HS response, viz. (i) most tolerant (CLN), (ii) moderately tolerant (Pusa Sadabahar, IIHR2274, Hisar Arun and IIHR2201), (iii) sensitive (Pusa Ruby, Pusa‐120 and Pusa Rohini) and (iv) highly sensitive (CA4) (Figure 1a).

events.event_label: HS associates_with seeded fruits
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p009.s015: CLN is reported to be tolerant to salt stress (Saeed et al.,) and TMV infection (https://avrdc.org). PMC7540533.p009.s016: CLN has been shown to have higher plant vigour and pollen viability as compared to sensitive CA4 (Sangu et al.,). PMC7540533.p009.s017: In addition, CLN is reported to maintain highest percentage of seeded fruits under HS as compared to several other cultivars (Comlekcioglu and Soylu,). PMC7540533.p009.s018: The CLN and CA4 pair has been used as donor parents for generating mapping populations towards HS (https://avrdc.org). PMC7540533.p009.s019: Thus, decoding the transcriptome of these two cultivars in HS response should provide valuable resource for future research to improve thermotolerance in tomato.

events.event_label: HS occurs_in leaf
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p014.s001: We next examined only that fraction of transcriptome that was significantly modulated (FDR P‐value ≤ 0.05 and fold change ≥2 and ≤−2 for up‐ and down‐regulated genes, respectively) in leaf under HS as depicted by volcano plots (Figure S2d). PMC7540533.p014.s002: A total of 6954 differentially expressing genes (DEGs) were identified between both cultivars (Figure 2a). PMC7540533.p014.s003: This number is the highest number of DEGs so far in tomato and the first for leaf utilizing contrasting cultivars (Bita et al.,; Fragkostefanakis et al.,; Fragkostefanakis et al.,; Keller et al.,). PMC7540533.p014.s004: A slightly higher number of HS‐responsive genes were present in the HS‐tolerant cultivar (5239 vs. 5138 in CLN and CA4, respectively); moreover, the up‐regulated events were more in tolerant cultivar [2580 (CLN) and 2496 (CA4)], while almost equal number of down‐regulated genes were present in both cultivars [2659 (CLN) and 2642 (CA4)] (Figure 2a).

events.event_label: HS associates_with HS response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p016.s001: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response. PMC7540533.p016.s002: GO enrichment analysis suggested the involvement of up‐regulated genes in cellular response to nutrient starvation specifically phosphate starvation in addition to protein folding (Figure S3a,b). PMC7540533.p016.s003: Furthermore, proteins belonging to chaperones, winged helix TFs, mRNA splicing and processing factors were significantly enriched among the conserved up‐regulated genes (Figure S3c). PMC7540533.p016.s004: KEGG pathways like protein processing in ER, spliceosome and ubiquitin‐mediated proteolysis were enriched in conserved up‐regulated genes (Table S3).

events.event_label: Notabilis decreases_abundance_of ABA
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p029.s008: This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst.

PMC7540533.p029.s012: This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS. PMC7540533.p029.s013: The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance.

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: The footprint of tomato cultivation, a cool region crop that exhibits heat stress (HS) sensitivity, is increasing in the tropics/sub‐tropics.

relations.evidence_preview: The footprint of tomato cultivation, a cool region crop that exhibits heat stress (HS) sensitivity, is increasing in the tropics/sub‐tropics.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p003.s001: The footprint of tomato cultivation, a cool region crop that exhibits heat stress (HS) sensitivity, is increasing in the tropics/sub‐tropics. PMC7540533.p003.s002: Knowledge of novel regulatory hot spots from varieties growing in the Indian sub‐continent climatic zones could be vital for developing HS‐resilient crops. PMC7540533.p003.s003: Comparative transcriptome‐wide signatures of a tolerant (CLN1621L) and sensitive (CA4) cultivar pair shortlisted from a pool of varieties exhibiting variable thermo‐sensitivity using physiological‐, survival‐ and yield‐related traits revealed redundant to cultivar‐specific HS regulation. PMC7540533.p003.s004: The antagonistically expressing genes encode enzymes and proteins that have roles in plant defence and abiotic stresses.

evidence_context_sentences.text: The footprint of tomato cultivation, a cool region crop that exhibits heat stress (HS) sensitivity, is increasing in the tropics/sub‐tropics.
Knowledge of novel regulatory hot spots from varieties growing in the Indian sub‐continent climatic zones could be vital for developing HS‐resilient crops.

Comparative transcriptome‐wide signatures of a tolerant (CLN1621L) and sensitive (CA4) cultivar pair shortlisted from a pool of varieties exhibiting variable thermo‐sensitivity using physiological‐, survival‐ and yield‐related traits revealed redundant to cultivar‐specific HS regulation.
The antagonistically expressing genes encode enzymes and proteins that have roles in plant defence and abiotic stresses.

relations.subject: HSFA1

evidence.text: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module.

Upon HS, HSFA1s, the master regulators of HSR, are relieved from repression by HSP70/90, relocate from cytoplasm to nucleus and activate other HSFs (HSFA7s, HSFA2 and HSFBs), dehydration‐responsive element‐binding protein 2A (DREB2A) and multiprotein bridging factor 1C (MBF1C) (Liu et al.,; Mishra et al.,; Yoshida et al.,).

These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,).
In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).
Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,).

evidence.text: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

relations.evidence_preview: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s014: Apart from these canonical HSR circuits, PIF4 regulatory and associated signalling networks control plant thermo‐morphogenesis (Koini et al.,; Oh et al.,; Proveniers and Van Zanten,; Stavang et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s015: The significance of post‐translational regulation in HS response is also well documented in plant HSR (Agarwal et al.,; Hahn et al.,; Miller et al.,; Mizoi et al.,; Sato et al.,; Vainonen et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s016: Evidences also suggest orchestration of multi‐layered regulatory systems comprising not only TFs but also epigenetic regulators and small RNAs as important HSR players.

evidence_context_sentences.text: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).
Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,).
Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,).
In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

Apart from these canonical HSR circuits, PIF4 regulatory and associated signalling networks control plant thermo‐morphogenesis (Koini et al.,; Oh et al.,; Proveniers and Van Zanten,; Stavang et al.,).

In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

Apart from these canonical HSR circuits, PIF4 regulatory and associated signalling networks control plant thermo‐morphogenesis (Koini et al.,; Oh et al.,; Proveniers and Van Zanten,; Stavang et al.,).

PMC7540533.p006.s013: CLN1621L (henceforth referred to as CLN, tolerant) and CA4 (sensitive). PMC7540533.p006.s014: Comparative analysis of the heat‐responsive transcriptomic signatures of leaves highlighted conserved to genotype‐specific reprogramming at transcriptional levels. PMC7540533.p006.s015: Functional assessment by silencing (virus‐induced gene silencing, VIGS) and transient overexpression of three previously uncharacterized genes, that exhibit antagonistic expression in the contrasting cultivars under HS, established their roles as positive/negative regulators of HS tolerance. evidence_context_sentences.text: One way for developing HS‐resilient crops is to exploit the naturally evolved mechanistic signalling frameworks in cultivars exhibiting contrasting response to HS (Challinor et al.,).

To uncover the HS‐responsive molecular mechanisms in tomato using naturally occurring cultivars growing in the sub‐tropical climatic conditions of the Indian sub‐continent, we first evaluated nine tolerant/sensitive tomato cultivars under HS to identify the best contrasting pair, viz.
CLN1621L (henceforth referred to as CLN, tolerant) and CA4 (sensitive).
Comparative analysis of the heat‐responsive transcriptomic signatures of leaves highlighted conserved to genotype‐specific reprogramming at transcriptional levels.

Functional assessment by silencing (virus‐induced gene silencing, VIGS) and transient overexpression of three previously uncharacterized genes, that exhibit antagonistic expression in the contrasting cultivars under HS, established their roles as positive/negative regulators of HS tolerance.

PMC7540533.p009.s018: The CLN and CA4 pair has been used as donor parents for generating mapping populations towards HS (https://avrdc.org). PMC7540533.p009.s019: Thus, decoding the transcriptome of these two cultivars in HS response should provide valuable resource for future research to improve thermotolerance in tomato.

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response.

relations.evidence_preview: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p016.s001: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response. PMC7540533.p016.s002: GO enrichment analysis suggested the involvement of up‐regulated genes in cellular response to nutrient starvation specifically phosphate starvation in addition to protein folding (Figure S3a,b). PMC7540533.p016.s003: Furthermore, proteins belonging to chaperones, winged helix TFs, mRNA splicing and processing factors were significantly enriched among the conserved up‐regulated genes (Figure S3c). PMC7540533.p016.s004: KEGG pathways like protein processing in ER, spliceosome and ubiquitin‐mediated proteolysis were enriched in conserved up‐regulated genes (Table S3).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response.
GO enrichment analysis suggested the involvement of up‐regulated genes in cellular response to nutrient starvation specifically phosphate starvation in addition to protein folding (Figure S3a,b).
Furthermore, proteins belonging to chaperones, winged helix TFs, mRNA splicing and processing factors were significantly enriched among the conserved up‐regulated genes (Figure S3c).
KEGG pathways like protein processing in ER, spliceosome and ubiquitin‐mediated proteolysis were enriched in conserved up‐regulated genes (Table S3).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Out of these, 402 TFs belonging to 46 families were found to be heat‐responsive in at least one cultivar.
In CLN and CA4, 314 (152 down/162 up) and 285 (152 down/133 up) TFs were significantly differentially regulated, respectively (Figure 3a, Table S4).
Similar HS response was evident for 201 (100 down/101 up) TFs in both cultivars (Figure 3a).
Thirty‐seven TFs (29 and 16 in CLN and CA4, respectively) exhibited very strong heat induction (FC > 10).
Thirty TFs followed conserved high (FC > 5) up‐regulation in both the cultivars, while 26 followed high (FC < −5) down‐regulation in both the cultivars.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Thirty‐seven TFs (29 and 16 in CLN and CA4, respectively) exhibited very strong heat induction (FC > 10).
Thirty TFs followed conserved high (FC > 5) up‐regulation in both the cultivars, while 26 followed high (FC < −5) down‐regulation in both the cultivars.

These highly transcribed TFs include members belonging to ERF (5 members), CO‐like (3 members), HSFs (4 TFs); 4 members each of MYB and C3H, C2C2‐CO‐like and NAC (2); and 1 each of B3, bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, Dof1, FAR1, HD‐ZIP1 and NF‐YB D (Figure 3b).
Of these, 3 TFs, viz.
HSFA2 (Solyc08g062960), and dehydration‐responsive element‐binding (DREB) TF (Solyc05g052410) were more than 100‐fold up‐regulated in both cultivars.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Thirty‐seven TFs (29 and 16 in CLN and CA4, respectively) exhibited very strong heat induction (FC > 10).
Thirty TFs followed conserved high (FC > 5) up‐regulation in both the cultivars, while 26 followed high (FC < −5) down‐regulation in both the cultivars.

These highly transcribed TFs include members belonging to ERF (5 members), CO‐like (3 members), HSFs (4 TFs); 4 members each of MYB and C3H, C2C2‐CO‐like and NAC (2); and 1 each of B3, bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, Dof1, FAR1, HD‐ZIP1 and NF‐YB D (Figure 3b).
Of these, 3 TFs, viz.
HSFA2 (Solyc08g062960), and dehydration‐responsive element‐binding (DREB) TF (Solyc05g052410) were more than 100‐fold up‐regulated in both cultivars.

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated.

evidence_context_sentences.text: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family.
Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4).
We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4.

Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated. HSFB1 is endowed with both co‐activator as well as repressor functions in tomato, while it acts as repressor in Arabidopsis (Bharti et al.,; Fragkostefanakis et al.,; Ikeda et al.,).

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s006: The Arabidopsis hsfb1‐hsfb2b double knockout mutant demonstrates higher acquired thermotolerance (Ikeda et al.,; Kumar et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s007: HSFA6a was up‐regulated, and HSFA4b was down‐regulated under HS only in CLN but not in CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s008: AtHSFA6a is induced in response to ABA, salt and drought and is responsible for the transcriptional activation of stress‐responsive gene DREB2a via ABA‐dependent signalling pathway (Hwang et al.,). evidence_context_sentences.text: HSFA6a was up‐regulated, and HSFA4b was down‐regulated under HS only in CLN but not in CA4 (Table S4).
AtHSFA6a is induced in response to ABA, salt and drought and is responsible for the transcriptional activation of stress‐responsive gene DREB2a via ABA‐dependent signalling pathway (Hwang et al.,).
Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,).
Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ).
Out of 147 ERFs reported in tomato, 30 members followed differential HS response in CLN and CA4 (Table S4).

evidence_context_sentences.text: HSFA6a was up‐regulated, and HSFA4b was down‐regulated under HS only in CLN but not in CA4 (Table S4).
AtHSFA6a is induced in response to ABA, salt and drought and is responsible for the transcriptional activation of stress‐responsive gene DREB2a via ABA‐dependent signalling pathway (Hwang et al.,).
Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,).
Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ).
Out of 147 ERFs reported in tomato, 30 members followed differential HS response in CLN and CA4 (Table S4).

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: We selected three previously uncharacterized ERFs (ERF.F4, ERF.F5, and ERF.C1) in HS response based on the observation that they all maintained high levels in CLN and low levels in CA4 under HS.

relations.evidence_preview: We selected three previously uncharacterized ERFs (ERF.F4, ERF.F5, and ERF.C1) in HS response based on the observation that they all maintained high levels in CLN and low levels in CA4 under HS.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s012: Of these, 12 ERFs were similarly regulated (9 up‐regulated and 3‐regulated down) in both cultivars, while others were regulated in cultivar‐biased manner. PMC7540533.p022.s013: We selected three previously uncharacterized ERFs (ERF.F4, ERF.F5, and ERF.C1) in HS response based on the observation that they all maintained high levels in CLN and low levels in CA4 under HS. PMC7540533.p022.s014: This was validated by qRT‐PCR also (Figure 3e). PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5.

evidence.text: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5.

relations.evidence_preview: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5. PMC7540533.p022.s017: Seedlings overexpressing ERF.F4 and F5 exhibited higher hypocotyl length as well as shoot, root and total seedling weight as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3g,h). PMC7540533.p022.s018: Our data on TF regulation dynamics highlight role of TF families other than the undisputed relevance of HSFs in HS response. PMC7540533.p022.s019: These TFs could be promising candidates for enhancing tomato thermotolerance as we demonstrated for the three ERFs that function as positive regulators of HS response.

Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.

relations.subject: Solyc09g014280

evidence.text: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,).

relations.evidence_preview: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,).

Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,).

Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,).
Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,).

Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,).

Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,).
Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s005: While Solyc09g014280 is induced upon potato cyst nematode infection in tomato roots (Swiecicka et al.,), Solyc05g052670 is up‐regulated in tomato in response to tomato yellow leaf curl virus infection (Chen et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s006: Here, we report yet unexplored function of an ASAT member in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p026.s007: Plants exhibiting successful knock‐down of Solyc09g014280 transcripts (Figure S11) showed severe drooping of leaves upon recovery after being exposed to HS in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4a).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,).
Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

While Solyc09g014280 is induced upon potato cyst nematode infection in tomato roots (Swiecicka et al.,), Solyc05g052670 is up‐regulated in tomato in response to tomato yellow leaf curl virus infection (Chen et al.,).
Here, we report yet unexplored function of an ASAT member in tomato HS response.

Plants exhibiting successful knock‐down of Solyc09g014280 transcripts (Figure S11) showed severe drooping of leaves upon recovery after being exposed to HS in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4a).

(g,h) Estimation of survival (percentage) and percentage reduction in hypocotyl length of seedlings under control conditions and after 5 days of heat stress in plants with overexpression of empty vector, ASAT and Notabilis seedlings.
Data are means of four biological sets of 70 seedlings each.

Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance.

When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a).
This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,).
Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,).

It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst. However, the leaves of Notabilis‐silenced plants show slightly increased ROS levels and HS‐induced cell death in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4b).
This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS.
The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Moreover, when we checked the expression of these HSR genes in response to ABA, they were all up‐regulated, suggesting Notabilis as an upstream regulator of these HS marker genes (Figure 4i).
Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance.
Furthermore, we asked whether the opposite expression of Notabilis in CLN‐CA4 cultivars is because of variation in promoter sequences and/or an inherent difference in TF pool of both the cultivars.
Sequence information of promoters for both the varieties highlighted no SNPs; thus, a regulatory influence of the cis‐sites in the contrasting expression was ruled out.
We then checked the expression of GUS gene hooked to Notabilis promoter in CLN and CA4 background.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Furthermore, another gene but a ‘sensitive‐up gene’ which is Solyc03g020030 was functionally characterized. This belongs to the proteinase inhibitor‐II (PI‐II) serine‐PI family having dimeric domains in the protein, inhibiting trypsin and chymotrypsin (Tamhane et al.,).
The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,).
Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,).

Only a Kunitz PI (another class of serine‐PI family) has been shown to be induced in response to heat stress (Annamalai and Yanagihara,), however, information regarding PI‐II‐class role in HS is completely lacking.

relations.subject: PI‐II

evidence.text: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c).

relations.evidence_preview: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). PMC7540533.p031.s010: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c). PMC7540533.p031.s011: In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d). PMC7540533.p031.s012: The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings. PMC7540533.p031.s013: In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g).

evidence_context_sentences.text: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c).
In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d).

The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings.
In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g).
Furthermore, enhanced expression of HS marker genes in PI‐II‐silenced plants confirmed its role as a negative regulator of HS tolerance (Figure 5h,i).

entities.selected_description: Any biological process that results in permanent cessation of all vital functions of a cell. A cell should be considered dead when any one of the following molecular or morphological criteria is met: (1) the cell has lost the integrity of its plasma membrane; (2) the cell, including its nucleus, has undergone complete fragmentation into discrete bodies (frequently referred to as apoptotic bodies). The cell corpse (or its fragments) may be engulfed by an adjacent cell in vivo, but engulfment of whole cells should not be considered a strict criteria to define cell death as, under some circumstances, live engulfed cells can be released from phagosomes (see PMID:18045538).

entities.selected_description: Any of a series of ribonucleoprotein complexes that contain snRNA(s) and small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs), and are formed sequentially during the spliceosomal splicing of one or more substrate RNAs, and which also contain the RNA substrate(s) from the initial target RNAs of splicing, the splicing intermediate RNA(s), to the final RNA products. During cis-splicing, the initial target RNA is a single, contiguous RNA transcript, whether mRNA, snoRNA, etc., and the released products are a spliced RNA and an excised intron, generally as a lariat structure. During trans-splicing, there are two initial substrate RNAs, the spliced leader RNA and a pre-mRNA.
